subject: Physics
q:
Two very long, straight, and insulated wires are kept at 90o angle from each other in xy-plane as shown in figure.

These wires carry currents of equal magnitude I , whose direction are shown in the figure. The net magnetic field at point P will be:
question:
Two very long, straight, and insulated wires are kept at 90o angle from each other in xy-plane as shown in figure.

These wires carry currents of equal magnitude I , whose direction are shown in the figure. The net magnetic field at point P will be:
solution:
Magnetic field due to wire along x-axis:
B1=μ0I2πdz^
Magnetic field due to wire along z-axis:
B2=μ0I2πd(-z^)
B→net=B→1+B→2=0

subject: Physics
q: A solenoid of length $0.5 \mathrm{~m}$ has a radius of $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ and is made up of ' $\mathrm{m}$ ' number of turns. It carries a current of $5 \mathrm{~A}$. If the magnitude of the magnetic field inside the solenoid is $6.28 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~T}$ then the value of $m$ is _____.
question: A solenoid of length $0.5 \mathrm{~m}$ has a radius of $1 \mathrm{~cm}$ and is made up of ' $\mathrm{m}$ ' number of turns. It carries a current of $5 \mathrm{~A}$. If the magnitude of the magnetic field inside the solenoid is $6.28 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~T}$ then the value of $m$ is _____.
solution:
$\mu_0 n i=B \quad n=$ number of turns per unit length
$\mu_0\left(\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\ell}\right) \mathrm{i}=\mathrm{B}$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{m}=\frac{B . \ell}{\mu_0 \mathrm{i}}=\frac{6.28 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5}{12.56 \times 10^{-7} \times 5} \\ & \mathrm{~m}=500\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The charged particle moving in a uniform magnetic field of $(3\hat{i} + 2\hat{j})$ T has an acceleration $\left(4\hat{i} - \dfrac{x}{2}\hat{j}\right)$ m/s$^2$. The value of $x$ is __________.
question: The charged particle moving in a uniform magnetic field of $(3\hat{i} + 2\hat{j})$ T has an acceleration $\left(4\hat{i} - \dfrac{x}{2}\hat{j}\right)$ m/s$^2$. The value of $x$ is __________.
solution:
The magnetic force on a moving charged particle is given by $\vec{F} = q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$.

Since the acceleration $\vec{a}$ is in the direction of the magnetic force, it must be perpendicular to the magnetic field $\vec{B}$.

Therefore, the dot product of acceleration and magnetic field is zero:

$\vec{a} \cdot \vec{B} = 0$

Substituting the given vectors:

$\left(4\hat{i} - \dfrac{x}{2}\hat{j}\right) \cdot (3\hat{i} + 2\hat{j}) = 0$

$(4)(3) + \left(-\dfrac{x}{2}\right)(2) = 0$

$12 - x = 0$

$x = 12$

Answer: $12$

subject: Physics
q: Consider a long thin conducting wire carrying a uniform current I. A particle having mass " M " and charge " $q$ " is released at a distance " $a$ " from the wire with a speed $v_0$ along the direction of current in the wire. The particle gets attracted to the wire due to magnetic force. The particle turns round when it is at distance $x$ from the wire. The value of $x$ is [ $\mu_0$ is vacuum permeability]
question: Consider a long thin conducting wire carrying a uniform current I. A particle having mass " M " and charge " $q$ " is released at a distance " $a$ " from the wire with a speed $v_0$ along the direction of current in the wire. The particle gets attracted to the wire due to magnetic force. The particle turns round when it is at distance $x$ from the wire. The value of $x$ is [ $\mu_0$ is vacuum permeability]
solution: $\begin{aligned} & A \rightarrow B \\ & \vec{V}=-v_x \hat{i}+v_y \hat{j} \\ & \vec{B}=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi r}(-\hat{k}) \\ & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=\mathrm{q}(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}})=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{Iq}}{2 \pi r}\left[-\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}} \hat{\mathrm{j}}-\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}} \hat{\mathrm{i}}\right] \\ & \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{x}}=-\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{Iq}}{2 \pi \mathrm{~m}} \cdot \frac{v_y}{\mathrm{r}} \\ & \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{y}}=-\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{Iq}}{2 \pi m} \cdot \frac{v_x}{\mathrm{r}}\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dv}_{\mathrm{x}}}{\mathrm{dr}}=-\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{Iq}}{2 \pi \mathrm{~m}} \frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}}{\mathrm{r}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dv}_{\mathrm{x}}}{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}}=-\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{Iq}}{2 \pi \mathrm{~m}} \frac{\mathrm{dr}}{\mathrm{r}} \\ & \int_0^{\mathrm{v}_0} \frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dv}_{\mathrm{x}}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{v}_0^2-\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}}^2}}=-\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{Iq}^{\mathrm{x}_1}}{2 \pi \mathrm{~m}} \int_{\mathrm{a}}^{\mathrm{dr}} \frac{\mathrm{r}}{2} \\ & \text { Let, } \mathrm{z}^2=\mathrm{v}_0^2-\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}}^2 \\ & 2 \mathrm{zdz}=-2 \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dv}_{\mathrm{x}}\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{zdz}=-\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dv}_{\mathrm{x}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dv}_x}{\sqrt{\mathrm{v}_0^2-\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}}^2}}=\frac{-\mathrm{zdz}}{\mathrm{z}}=-\mathrm{dz} \end{aligned}$ then integral becomes $\begin{aligned} & -\int_{\mathrm{v}_0}^0 \mathrm{dz}=-\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{Iq}}{2 \pi \mathrm{~m}} \ln \frac{\mathrm{x}_1}{\mathrm{a}} \\ & \mathrm{v}_0=-\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{Iq}}{2 \pi \mathrm{~m}} \ln \frac{\mathrm{x}_1}{\mathrm{a}} \end{aligned}$ $\left.\mathrm{x}_1=\mathrm{a} \mathrm{e}^{-\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{mv}}{\mu_0}} \mathrm{H}_0 \mathrm{q} \right\rvert\, \ldots \ldots$ For B $\rightarrow$ C $\begin{aligned} & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}}=-v_x \hat{\mathrm{i}}-v_y \hat{\mathrm{j}} \\ & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{~B}}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}}(-\hat{\mathrm{k}}) \\ & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}=\mathrm{q}(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{v}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}})=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{Iq}}{2 \pi r}\left(-v_x \hat{j}+v_y \hat{\mathrm{i}}\right) \end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{x}}=+\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{Iq}}{2 \pi \mathrm{~m}} \frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}}{\mathrm{r}} \quad \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{y}}=-\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{Iq}}{2 \pi \mathrm{~m}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}}}{\mathrm{r}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dv}_{\mathrm{x}}}{\mathrm{dr}}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{Iq}}{2 \pi \mathrm{~m}} \frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{y}}}{\mathrm{r}} \\ & \int_{v_0}^0 \frac{\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{dv}_{\mathrm{x}}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{v}_0^2-\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{x}}^2}}=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{Iq}}{2 \pi \mathrm{~m}} \int_{\mathrm{x_1}}^{\mathrm{x}}\frac{dr}{r} \end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{Iq}}{2 \pi \mathrm{~m}} \ln \frac{\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}_1}=-\int_0^{\mathrm{v}_0} \mathrm{~d} \mathrm{z}=-\mathrm{v}_0 \\ & \mathrm{x}=\mathrm{x}_1 \mathrm{e}^{-\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{~m}_0}{\mu_0 \mathrm{Iq}}} \ldots \ldots \text { (2) } \end{aligned}$ From equation 1 and 2 $X=a e^{-\frac{4 \pi \mathrm{mv}_0}{\mu_0 \mathrm{Iq}}}$

subject: Physics
q: A long solenoid with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>turns</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> has a core material with relative permeability <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn></math> and volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If the core material is replaced by another material having relative permeability of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>750</mn></math> with same volume maintaining same current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>75</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> in the solenoid, the fractional change in the magnetic moment of the core would be approximately <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>499</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> Find the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A long solenoid with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn><mo> </mo><mi>turns</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> has a core material with relative permeability <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn></math> and volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>3</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></msup><mo>.</mo></math> If the core material is replaced by another material having relative permeability of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>750</mn></math> with same volume maintaining same current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>75</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> in the solenoid, the fractional change in the magnetic moment of the core would be approximately <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>499</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math> Find the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
M→=magnetic moment m→V
⇒ χH→=m→V
⇒ m→=μr-1NiV
∴ Δmm=[750-1-500-1]NiV500-1NiV=250

subject: Physics
q: Two long straight wires <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> carrying equal current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> each were kept parallel to each other at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> distance. Magnitude of magnetic force experienced by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> length of wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mn mathvariant="italic">1</mn></msub></math>. If distance between wires is halved and currents on them are doubled, force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> length of wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> will be:
question: Two long straight wires <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> carrying equal current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> each were kept parallel to each other at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> distance. Magnitude of magnetic force experienced by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> length of wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mn mathvariant="italic">1</mn></msub></math>. If distance between wires is halved and currents on them are doubled, force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>F</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> length of wire <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> will be:
solution:
As we know, force per unit length between two parallel straight wires is given by,  F=μ0i1i22πd.

Therefore, required ratio

F1 F2=μ0(10)22π(5×10-2)μ0(20)22π52×10-2=18

⇒F2=8F1

subject: Physics
q: A moving coil galvanometer has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> turns and each turn has an area of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The magnetic field produced by the magnet is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> and the deflection in the coil is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn></math> radian when a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math> is passed through it. The torsional constant of the suspension wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>rad</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______ .
question: A moving coil galvanometer has <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn></math> turns and each turn has an area of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The magnetic field produced by the magnet is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>01</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> and the deflection in the coil is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn></math> radian when a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mA</mi></math> is passed through it. The torsional constant of the suspension wire is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>rad</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______ .
solution:
Torque produced in a moving coil galvanometer is, τ=BINAsinϕ.

Let torsional constant of the suspension wire be C, then Cθ=BINAsin90°

⇒C= BINA θ=0.01×10×10-3×100×2×10-40.05

=4×10-5 N-m/rad.

Therefore, x=4.

subject: Physics
q: An iron rod of volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> and relative permeability <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn></math> is placed as core in a solenoid with<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>turns</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . If a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is passed through the solenoid, then the magnetic moment of the rod will be :
question: An iron rod of volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> and relative permeability <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn></math> is placed as core in a solenoid with<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>turns</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>cm</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> . If a current of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> is passed through the solenoid, then the magnetic moment of the rod will be :
solution:
Given,

Volume of iron rod is, V=10-3 m3

Relative permeability, μr=1000

No of turns per unit length of solenoid is, n=Nl=10 turns cm-1=1000 turns m-1

Current through the solenoid is, i=0.5 A

Magnetic moment of an iron core solenoid is given by,

M=(μr-1) NiA

⇒M=μr-1NiVl=μr-1NliV

⇒M=1000-1×1000×0.5×10-3

⇒M=499.5≈500 A m2

x>d path is straight line

-y-12at2x-d=atV0

-y-12at2at=x-dV0

-yat-12dV0=xV0-dV0

-myV0qEd=xV0-d2V0

y=-qEdmV0xV0-d2V0

y=qEdmV02d2-x.

independent of its
question:

solution: $\mathrm{T}=2 \pi \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{Bq}$

subject: Physics
q: A charge particle moves along circular path in a uniform magnetic field in a cyclotron. The kinetic energy of the charge particle increases to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> times its initial value. What will be the ratio of new radius to the original radius of circular path of the charge particle?
question: A charge particle moves along circular path in a uniform magnetic field in a cyclotron. The kinetic energy of the charge particle increases to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> times its initial value. What will be the ratio of new radius to the original radius of circular path of the charge particle?
solution:
Radius of a charged particle moving in a magnetic field perpendicular to its velocity is given by, R=mvqB.

mv can be written in terms of kinetic energy as mv=2mKE. Therefore,

R=mvqB=2mKEqB

⇒R∝KE⇒R2R1=K2K1=41=21

subject: Physics
q: A long straight wire of circular cross-section (radius a) is carrying steady current I. The current I is uniformly distributed across this cross-section. The magnetic field is
question: A long straight wire of circular cross-section (radius a) is carrying steady current I. The current I is uniformly distributed across this cross-section. The magnetic field is
options: inversely proportional to r in the region r<a and uniform throughout in the region  r>a
directly proportional to r in the region r<a and inversely proportional to r in the region r>a

Zero in the region <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo><</mo><mi>a</mi></math> and inversely proportional to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> in the region <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mo>></mo><mi>a</mi></math>
uniform in the region r<a and inversely proportional to distance r from the axis, in the region r>a
solution:
The formula to calculate the magnetic field inside the conducting wire is given by

Bin =μ0Jr2   ...(1)

The formula to calculate the magnetic field outside the conducting wire is given by

Bout =μ0Ja22r   ...(2)

From equation (1), it can be concluded that the magnetic field is directly proportional to the radial distance inside the wire, whereas from equation (2), it can be concluded that the magnetic field is inversely proportional to the radial distance outside the wire.

question:

(Assume that the current is flowing in the clockwise direction.)
solution:
B1=μ0I4πR2sin60=4π×10-7×1.54π.332×10-2×2×32=1×10-5
Bnet=3B1=3×10-5T⊗

subject: Physics
q: An electric current is flowing through a circular coil of radius $\mathrm{R}$. The ratio of the magnetic field at the centre of the coil and that at a distance $2 \sqrt{2} R$ from the centre of the coil and on its axis is :
question: An electric current is flowing through a circular coil of radius $\mathrm{R}$. The ratio of the magnetic field at the centre of the coil and that at a distance $2 \sqrt{2} R$ from the centre of the coil and on its axis is :
solution:
Given $:$ Radius $=R$
Distance $\mathrm{x}=2 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{R}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{B_{\text {centre }}}{B_{\text {axis }}} & =\left(1+\frac{x^2}{R^2}\right)^{3 / 2}=\left(1+\frac{(2 \sqrt{2} R)^2}{R^2}\right)^{3 / 2} \\
& =(9)^{3 / 2}=27
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: Figure shows a current carrying square loop ABCD of edge length is ' $a$ ' lying in a plane. If the resistance of the $A B C$ part is $r$ and that of $A D C$ part is 2 r , then the magnitude of the resultant magnetic field at centre of the square loop is
question: Figure shows a current carrying square loop ABCD of edge length is ' $a$ ' lying in a plane. If the resistance of the $A B C$ part is $r$ and that of $A D C$ part is 2 r , then the magnitude of the resultant magnetic field at centre of the square loop is
solution:
$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{\mathrm{AB}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{\mathrm{BC}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{\mathrm{CD}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}_{\mathrm{DA}}$
$\begin{aligned} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}= & {\left[\frac{-\mu_0(2 \mathrm{I} / 3)}{4 \pi(\mathrm{a} / 2)} \sqrt{2}-\frac{\mu_0(2 \mathrm{I} / 3)}{4 \pi(\mathrm{a} / 2)} \sqrt{2}\right.} \\ & \left.+\frac{\mu_0(\mathrm{I} / 3)}{4 \pi(\mathrm{a} / 2)} \sqrt{2}+\frac{\mu_0(\mathrm{I} / 3)}{4 \pi(\mathrm{a} / 2)} \sqrt{2}\right] \hat{\mathrm{k}} \\ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}= & {\left[\frac{-2 \sqrt{2} \mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{3 \pi \mathrm{a}}+\frac{\sqrt{2} \mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{3 \pi \mathrm{a}}\right] \hat{\mathrm{k}} } \\ \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}= & \frac{-\sqrt{2} \mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{3 \pi \mathrm{a}} \hat{\mathrm{k}}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A conducting metal circular-wire-loop of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is placed perpendicular to a magnetic field which varies with time as <math><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><msup><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>τ</mi></mrow></mfrac></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mi> </mi><mi>and</mi><mi> </mi><mi>τ</mi></math> are constants at time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. If the resistance of the loop is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, then the heat generated in the loop after a long time <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>∞</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is
question: A conducting metal circular-wire-loop of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is placed perpendicular to a magnetic field which varies with time as <math><mi>B</mi><mo>=</mo><msub><mrow><mi>B</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><msup><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>τ</mi></mrow></mfrac></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mi> </mi><mi>and</mi><mi> </mi><mi>τ</mi></math> are constants at time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. If the resistance of the loop is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, then the heat generated in the loop after a long time <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>t</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>∞</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is
solution:
Here, B=B0e-tτ
Area of the circular loop, A=πr2
Flux linked with the loop at any time  t ,
ϕ=BA=πr2B0e-tτ 
Emf induced in the loop, ε=-dϕdt=πr2B01τe-tτ
Net heat generated in the loop
∫0∞ε2Rdt=π2r4B02τ2R∫0∞e-2tτdt
=π2r4B02τ2R×1-2τ×e-2tτ0∞
=-π2r4B022τ2R×τ0-1=π2r4B022τR

subject: Physics
q: The magnetic field at the center of current carrying circular loop is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. The magnetic field at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></math> times radius of the given circular loop from the center on its axis is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> will be
question: The magnetic field at the center of current carrying circular loop is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. The magnetic field at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></math> times radius of the given circular loop from the center on its axis is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> will be
solution:
Magnetic field at the centre of the loop i.e. B1=μ0i2R

Magnetic field on the axis of the loop is,

B2=μ0iR22R2+x232=μ0i×R22R2+3R232=μ0i×R22×8R3=μ0i2×8R

Therefore, the ratio B1B2=81

subject: Physics
q:
The current sensitivity of a galvanometer can be increased by :
(A) decreasing the number of turns
(B) increasing the magnetic field
(C) decreasing the area of the coil
(D) decreasing the torsional constant of the spring

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
The current sensitivity of a galvanometer can be increased by :
(A) decreasing the number of turns
(B) increasing the magnetic field
(C) decreasing the area of the coil
(D) decreasing the torsional constant of the spring

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
solution:
The current in the galvanometer is calculated by i=KNABθ.

Therefore, the sensitivity of the galvanometer,

dθdi=NABK

Hence, only options B and D are correct.

subject: Physics
q: A current of $1 \mathrm{~A}$ is flowing on the sides of an equilateral triangle of side $4.5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$. The magnetic field at the centre of the triangle will be:
question: A current of $1 \mathrm{~A}$ is flowing on the sides of an equilateral triangle of side $4.5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$. The magnetic field at the centre of the triangle will be:
solution:
Here, side of the triangle, $l=4.5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$, current, $\mathrm{I}=1 \mathrm{~A}$ magnetic field at the centre of the triangle ' $O$ ' $B=$ ? From figure, $\tan 60^{\circ}=\sqrt{3}=\frac{1}{2 d}$ $$ \Rightarrow d=\frac{l}{2 \sqrt{3}}=\left(\frac{4.5 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \sqrt{3}}\right) \mathrm{m} $$
Magnetic field, $B=\frac{\mu_0 i}{4 \pi d}\left(\cos \theta_1+\cos \theta_2\right)$ Putting value of $\mu=4 \pi \times 10^{-7}$ and $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ we will get net magenetic field $=3 \times B=4 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~Wb} / \mathrm{m}^2$

subject: Physics
q:
The region between $y=0$ and $y=\mathrm{d}$ contains a magnetic field $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}=\mathrm{B} \hat{\mathrm{z}}$. A particle of mass $\mathrm{m}$ and charge $\mathrm{q}$ enters

the region with a velocity $\vec{v}=v \hat{i} .$ if $\mathrm{d}=\frac{\mathrm{m} v}{2 \mathrm{qB}},$ the acceleration of the charged particle at the point of its emergence at the other side is :
question:
The region between $y=0$ and $y=\mathrm{d}$ contains a magnetic field $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}=\mathrm{B} \hat{\mathrm{z}}$. A particle of mass $\mathrm{m}$ and charge $\mathrm{q}$ enters

the region with a velocity $\vec{v}=v \hat{i} .$ if $\mathrm{d}=\frac{\mathrm{m} v}{2 \mathrm{qB}},$ the acceleration of the charged particle at the point of its emergence at the other side is :
solution:
Assuming particle enters from $(0, \mathrm{~d})$ $r=\frac{m v}{q B}, \quad d=\frac{r}{2}$
$a=\frac{q V B}{m}\left[\frac{-\sqrt{3} i-j}{2}\right]$ this option is not given in the all above four choices.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements : One is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).

Assertion (A) : In an uniform magnetic field, speed and energy remains the same for a moving charged particle.

Reason (R): Moving charged particle experiences magnetic force perpendicular to its direction of motion.
question:
Given below are two statements : One is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).

Assertion (A) : In an uniform magnetic field, speed and energy remains the same for a moving charged particle.

Reason (R): Moving charged particle experiences magnetic force perpendicular to its direction of motion.
options: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion.
Assertion is true but Reason is false.
Assertion is false but Reason is true.
solution:
The force experienced by the charge particle is perpendicular to the instantaneous velocity at all instants as F→=qv→×B→.

Hence, the magnetic force cannot bring any change in the speed of the charged particle. Since, the speed remains constant, the kinetic energy also remains constant.

subject: Physics
q: If in a circular coil $A$ of radius $R$, current I is flowing and in another coil $B$ of radius $2 R$ a current $2 \mathrm{l}$ is flowing, then the ratio of the magnetic fields $\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{B}}$, produced by them will be
question: If in a circular coil $A$ of radius $R$, current I is flowing and in another coil $B$ of radius $2 R$ a current $2 \mathrm{l}$ is flowing, then the ratio of the magnetic fields $\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{A}}$ and $\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{B}}$, produced by them will be
solution: In coil A, B $=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 \pi \mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{R}} . \quad \therefore \mathrm{B} \alpha \frac{\mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{R}}$; Hence, $\frac{\mathrm{B}_1}{\mathrm{~B}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{I}_1}{\mathrm{R}_1} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{R}_2}{\mathrm{I}_2}=\frac{2}{2}=1$

q: Find the magnetic field at point P due to a straight line segment AB of length 6 cm carrying a current of 5 A. (See figure) μ0=4π×10-7 NA-2
question: Find the magnetic field at point P due to a straight line segment AB of length 6 cm carrying a current of 5 A. (See figure) μ0=4π×10-7 NA-2
solution:
θ=370
r=4 cm
B=μ0i4πr [sin37°+sin37°]
=4π×10-754π×4×10-235+35
=32×10-5=1.5×10-5 T

subject: Physics
q:
An electron is allowed to move with constant velocity along the axis of current carrying straight solenoid.
(A) The electron will experience magnetic force along the axis of the solenoid.
(B) The electron will not experience magnetic force.
(C) The electron will continue to move along the axis of the solenoid.
(D) The electron will be accelerated along the axis of the solenoid.
(E) The electron will follow parabolic path-inside the solenoid.
Choose the correct answer from the option given below:
question:
An electron is allowed to move with constant velocity along the axis of current carrying straight solenoid.
(A) The electron will experience magnetic force along the axis of the solenoid.
(B) The electron will not experience magnetic force.
(C) The electron will continue to move along the axis of the solenoid.
(D) The electron will be accelerated along the axis of the solenoid.
(E) The electron will follow parabolic path-inside the solenoid.
Choose the correct answer from the option given below:
solution:
The Lorentz force is given by F→=q(v→×B→)=qvBsinθn^. The angle θ=0°. Hence the force experienced is zero.

Thus, the electron will not experience any magnetic force and the electron will continue to move along the axis.

q: Radiation, with wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6561</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Å</mi></math> falls on a metal surface to produce photoelectrons. The electrons are made to enter a uniform magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> . If the radius of the largest circular path followed by the electrons is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> , the work function of the metal is close to:
question: Radiation, with wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6561</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Å</mi></math> falls on a metal surface to produce photoelectrons. The electrons are made to enter a uniform magnetic field of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> . If the radius of the largest circular path followed by the electrons is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> , the work function of the metal is close to:
solution:
KEmax=E-ϕ
=12400λinÅ-ϕ (in eV)
∴r=2mKEeB
KEmax=r2e2B22m (in J)
=r2eB22m (in eV)
∴ϕ=124006556-r2eB22m
=1.1 eV

subject: Physics
q: A particle of charge $-16 \times 10^{-18}$ coulomb moving with velocity $10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ along the $\mathrm{x}$-axis enters a region where a magnetic field of induction $\mathrm{B}$ is along the $\mathrm{y}$-axis, and an electric field of magnitude $10^4 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$ is along the negative z-axis. If the charged particle continues moving along the $\mathrm{x}$-axis, the magnitude of $\mathrm{B}$ is
question: A particle of charge $-16 \times 10^{-18}$ coulomb moving with velocity $10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ along the $\mathrm{x}$-axis enters a region where a magnetic field of induction $\mathrm{B}$ is along the $\mathrm{y}$-axis, and an electric field of magnitude $10^4 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$ is along the negative z-axis. If the charged particle continues moving along the $\mathrm{x}$-axis, the magnitude of $\mathrm{B}$ is
solution: Since there is no deviation in the path of the charged particle, so net force due to presence of electric and magnetic field must be zero $\Rightarrow \mathrm{vB}=\mathrm{qE} \Rightarrow \mathrm{B}=\frac{\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{10^4}{10}=10^3 \mathrm{~Wb} / \mathrm{m}^2$

subject: Physics
q: Two magnetic dipoles X and Y are placed at a separation d , with their axes perpendicular to each other. The dipole moment of Y is twice that of X . A particle of charge q is passing through their mid-point P , at angle θ=45o with the horizontal line, as shown in figure. What would be the magnitude of force on the particle at that instant? ( d is much larger than the dimension of the dipole)
question: Two magnetic dipoles X and Y are placed at a separation d , with their axes perpendicular to each other. The dipole moment of Y is twice that of X . A particle of charge q is passing through their mid-point P , at angle θ=45o with the horizontal line, as shown in figure. What would be the magnitude of force on the particle at that instant? ( d is much larger than the dimension of the dipole)
solution:
BX=2μ04πMd/2 along x -axis
BY=μ04π2Md/2 along y -axis
BX=BY

Hence Bnet will be at 45o from x -axis.
Now, velocity and magnetic field are in same direction. Hence no force will act on the particle.

subject: Physics
q: Find the magnetic field at the point P in figure. The curved portion is a semicircle connected to two long straight wires.
question: Find the magnetic field at the point P in figure. The curved portion is a semicircle connected to two long straight wires.
solution:
Magnitude of magnetic field due to semi-infinite portion of wire AB at point C is

B1=μ0i4πr

Magnitude of magnetic field due to semicircular arc BD at point C is

B2=μ0i4r

Magnitude of magnetic field due to semi-infinite portion DE at point C is 0.

Using right-hand rule, it can be seen that directions of B1→ and B2→ are both outwards from the plane.

Therefore, the magnitude of net magnetic field at point C is

B=B1+B2

   =μ0i2r12+12π

subject: Physics
q:
A small bar magnet is moved through a coil at constant speed from one end to the other. Which of the following series of observations will be seen on the galvanometer G attached across the coil?

 

Three positions shown describe: (a) the magnet's entry (b) magnet is completely inside and (c) magnet's exit.
question:
A small bar magnet is moved through a coil at constant speed from one end to the other. Which of the following series of observations will be seen on the galvanometer G attached across the coil?

 

Three positions shown describe: (a) the magnet's entry (b) magnet is completely inside and (c) magnet's exit.
solution:
(a) When the bar magnet entering the coil the magnetic flux link with the coil increases, and according to the Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the emf will induce in the coil, which in tern induces current and galvanometer shows the deflection towards right.

(b) When the bar magnet is inside the coil no change in magnetic flux link with the coil, therefore galvanometer do not show deflection.

(c) When the bar magnet exit from the coil the magnetic flux link with the coil decreases, and according to the Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction, the emf will induce in the coil, in opposite direction which in tern induces current and galvanometer shows the deflection towards left.

subject: Physics
q: A long straight wire with a circular cross-section having radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, is carrying a steady current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math>. The current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> is uniformly distributed across this cross-section. Then the variation of magnetic field due to current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> with distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>r</mi><mo><</mo><mi>R</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> from its centre will be
question: A long straight wire with a circular cross-section having radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, is carrying a steady current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math>. The current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> is uniformly distributed across this cross-section. Then the variation of magnetic field due to current <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> with distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>r</mi><mo><</mo><mi>R</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> from its centre will be
solution:
Using Ampere's law and noting that the steady current is uniformly distributed over the cross-section of the cylindrical wire.

B=μ0Ir2πR2,   r≤Rμ0I2πr,   r>R

Thus, magnetic field B∝r from the centre for r<R.

subject: Physics
q: An electron moves through a uniform magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. At a particular instant of time, the velocity of electron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>u</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the magnetic force acting on electron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>e</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>e</mi></math> is the charge of electron, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is ____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>.
question: An electron moves through a uniform magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>B</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>0</mn></msub><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>. At a particular instant of time, the velocity of electron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>u</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the magnetic force acting on electron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover><mi>F</mi><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mi>e</mi><mover><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>e</mi></math> is the charge of electron, then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></math> is ____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math>.
solution:
Force acting on a charged particle under the magnetic field is given by, F→=qv→×B→.

⇒5ek⏜=e3i⏜+5j⏜×B0i⏜+2B0j⏜

⇒5ek⏜=e6B0k⏜-5B0k⏜

⇒B0=5 T

subject: Physics
q: A rigid square loop of side ‘a’ and carrying current I2 is lying on a horizontal surface near a long current I1 carrying wire in the same plane as shown in figure. The net force on the loop due to the wire will be:
question: A rigid square loop of side ‘a’ and carrying current I2 is lying on a horizontal surface near a long current I1 carrying wire in the same plane as shown in figure. The net force on the loop due to the wire will be:
solution:
Force on segment 1-2 and 3-4 will get cancel out.
Force on segment 4-1,
F1=μ0I1I22πa×a toward Right
Force on segment 2-3:
F2=μ0I1I22π(2a)×a  toward left
Net force on loop:
Fnet=F1-F2 toward Right
=μ0I1I22π-μ0I1I24π
=μ0I1I24π toward Right or repulsive
